topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp autumn air seemed to bite sharper than usual as I stood outside the gymnasium doors. The cheers from the team inside washed over me, a wave of sound that didn't quite reach the space where I stood. I clutched the worn strap of my duffel bag, the leather digging into my palm. My chest felt tight, a constricted space where excitement should have been. I watched the players move, their synchronized movements a fluid ballet I was clearly not part of. A deep, slow breath escaped me, shaky and uneven. "Guess it's not time yet," I murmured to myself, the sound swallowed by the echoing hallway. Every step I took toward the exit felt heavy, weighted down by a sudden, profound sense of disappointment.

[story 2]

I reread the email for the tenth time, the screen light reflecting dully in my eyes. The words confirmed what I already suspected: I hadn't made the roster. A strange numbness settled over my limbs, making it difficult to move. I walked down to the edge of the field where the training session had ended, the grass cool beneath my sneakers. I stared at the distant figures practicing drills, feeling an odd detachment. My jaw tightened involuntarily, and I bit down hard on the inside of my cheek until I tasted metal. "Of course," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle. The world seemed muted, the vibrant energy of the athletic environment suddenly distant and irrelevant.

[story 3]

The handshake felt strangely cold, the grip fleeting and impersonal. As the team gathered for the post-practice debrief, I kept my gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the polished floor, refusing to meet anyone's eyes. When the coach finally called my name, a polite nod was all I offered, my voice surprisingly steady, though internally, a tremor ran through me. I managed a small, tight smile, but it didn't reach my eyes. Later, sitting alone on the bench, I traced patterns on the wooden seat with my fingertip. The silence around me amplified the thumping of my own pulse in my ears. I pulled my knees up to my chest, wrapping my arms around them, the tension in my shoulders refusing to release.

I reread the email for the tenth time, the screen light reflecting dully in my eyes. The words confirmed what I already suspected: I hadn't made the roster.
